title: Requirement record ingestion missing — blocks entire task-graph pipeline
description: build_task_context (internal/taskcontext/taskcontext.go:183-189) and submit_task_graph (internal/tasks/tasks.go:308-311) both hard-require store.Get(requirement_id) to succeed, but no tool handler and no CLI command anywhere creates that record. internal/reconcile/reconcile.go:35 (CheckSourceStale) exists for this purpose but has zero callers outside tests — dead code. Result: any fresh Jira ticket cannot enter the task-graph pipeline at all; day-one blocker for the entire workflow feature.
acceptance_criteria: A tool (e.g. a new create/ingest tool) or CLI command creates a requirement record from a Jira issue (or manual input) that store.Get(requirement_id) can retrieve; internal/reconcile is wired in or removed; build_task_context/submit_task_graph succeed end-to-end for a fresh ticket without manual DB seeding.
close_reason: added ingest_jira_requirement tool; build_task_context/submit_task_graph descriptions + not-found errors now name the prerequisite too

title: Migrate installed Atlassian token keys to direct REST
description: Existing Punakawan installations may retain ATLASSIAN_MCP_TOKEN in both the global credential file and adapter env_passthrough, causing the restarted direct REST adapter to report ATLASSIAN_API_TOKEN missing. Add a recoverable installer migration and apply it to the current installation.
acceptance_criteria: Installer renames the legacy token key without exposing or changing its value, derives scoped mode from credential type, updates global adapter passthrough, creates backups, remains idempotent, passes syntax checks, and the current Codex installation can read Jira after restart.
close_reason: Installer now migrates legacy ATLASSIAN_MCP_TOKEN credentials and env_passthrough to direct REST keys with recoverable backups, derives scoped mode from whether an email is present, and is idempotent. Applied migration to current installation, reran installer twice, doctor passed, and live direct Jira issue plus JQL reads returned HTTP 200.

title: Replace Rovo MCP with direct Jira REST transport
description: The Atlassian adapter currently sends Jira operations through the hosted Rovo MCP endpoint, requiring organization-level Rovo permissions even though Jira Cloud REST API v3 supports direct API-token authentication. Replace the transport while preserving Punakawan operation contracts and approval behavior.
design: Keep the adapter RPC manifest stable so core and MCP clients do not change. Implement a Jira REST client behind the existing operation functions. Use site URL plus Basic email:token for unscoped personal tokens; use api.atlassian.com/ex/jira/{cloudId} for scoped tokens/service accounts. Preserve one-per-run Punakawan write approvals outside the adapter.
acceptance_criteria: All Jira read/search/create/edit/comment/worklog/transition/metadata operations use Jira REST API v3; personal unscoped tokens and scoped/service-account tokens select the appropriate authentication and base URL; installer and documentation no longer require Rovo; tests cover request mapping, authentication, errors, and all existing operation handlers; repository quality gates pass.
close_reason: Replaced hosted Rovo MCP transport with direct Jira Cloud REST v3 and Confluence REST. Added unscoped personal, scoped personal, and scoped service-account token modes; direct endpoint/auth/error handling; ADF write mapping; enhanced JQL search; request-level tests; installer migration; documentation and ADR updates. Verified 49 adapter tests, full repository tests, lint, build, package, and live read-only issue plus JQL calls against the configured Jira account.

title: Negotiate live Atlassian MCP tools and diagnose missing Jira reads
description: A real call to atlassian.getJiraIssue reaches mcp.atlassian.com but fails because the remote server reports Tool getJiraIssue not found. Official supported-tools documentation still lists getJiraIssue. Live tools/list shows only Teamwork Graph tools, and the only plausible graph read explicitly rejects this account with an API-token permission error. The adapter must inspect tools/list, distinguish permission-filtered tools from renamed/unavailable tools, and provide actionable configuration diagnostics without attempting an incompatible fallback.
design: Do not route Jira operations through Teamwork Graph tools: the live graph endpoint rejects the same API-token authorization and its generic object schema is not a proven semantic substitute for getJiraIssue. Capability negotiation is fail-fast and descriptive. Restarting Punakawan refreshes the cached list after admin access changes.
acceptance_criteria: Adapter discovers and caches remote tool names/schemas; canonical tools run when advertised; missing tools report the required Rovo MCP permission group and product scope plus the exact advertised tools; API-token rejection explains the Atlassian admin remedy; fake-server tests cover canonical, unavailable, token-rejected, and discovery cases; live configured-account tools/list validates the diagnosis without exposing credentials.
close_reason: Implemented live Atlassian MCP tools/list discovery and caching, fail-fast permission/scope diagnostics, API-token rejection translation, installer and README guidance, and fake-server coverage. Verified against the configured account: only Teamwork Graph tools are advertised and getTeamworkGraphObject rejects API-token access, confirming there is no safe Jira fallback. Adapter typecheck, 46 tests, build, installer syntax, and diff checks pass.

title: advance_workflow has no state-sequence validation
description: internal/workflow/workflow.go:67-84 Advance() only checks the target state is a recognized enum value and the run isn't already terminal — it does not validate the transition is reachable from the current state. Commit 1ccdac4 (internal/mcpserver/tools_workflow.go:63-108) added a check that blocks completion while unresolved Bagong findings exist, but that's orthogonal — it does not stop e.g. created->completed in one call, skipping context-building/reviewing entirely. Right now this is a timestamped note log wearing an enum's clothes, not an enforced state machine.
acceptance_criteria: Advance() (or its caller) enforces a defined transition graph — illegal jumps (e.g. skipping context-building/reviewing) are rejected with a clear error naming the missing intermediate state(s). Existing blocking-findings check on completion stays as an additional guard, not a replacement.
close_reason: Advance() now enforces §9's transition graph (forwardTransitions) instead of accepting any non-terminal next state; blocked/failed/cancelled remain universal escape hatches

title: Adapter-approval gate is self-approvable by the requesting agent
description: cmd/punakawan/approvals_cmd.go:11-17 documents the CLI-not-MCP-tool design specifically so 'the same connected AI client that requested an action also grant it to itself' cannot happen — but internal/approvals/approvals.go:126-143 Resolve() and internal/gitops/worktree.go:107-123 resolve() never check that approvedBy differs from RequestedBy. cmd/punakawan/approvals_cmd.go's approve subcommand takes an arbitrary --by string with no auth; cmd/punakawan/worktree_cmd.go:56-68 hardcodes approvedBy='cli-user'. An agent with shell access can request as 'semar' then approve as 'semar' in the same session, defeating the gate entirely.
acceptance_criteria: Resolve()/resolve() reject an approval where approvedBy == RequestedBy (or more generally where approver identity cannot be distinguished from the requesting role), OR the CLI approve path requires an out-of-band credential/context the agent's shell does not have. Add a test that a same-identity self-approval is rejected.
close_reason: Resolve() now rejects an agent role name (semar/gareng/petruk/bagong) as its own approver; documented as a floor not real auth - MCP elicitation remains the actual human-in-the-loop boundary

title: adapter-atlassian: literal HTML entities (e.g. ->) rendered in Jira comment text
description: After the ADF-rendering fix (punokawan-9ko), a live comment on TRF-21481 still showed "->" literally instead of "->" - the ADF structure (headings/lists/bold/code) was correct, only inline HTML entities leaked through. Reproduced directly: markdownToAdf("... -> ...") itself does NOT produce ">" - the source text handed to the adapter already contained the literal 4-character entity before it reached this package. Root cause of WHY the upstream caller sent already-escaped text is outside this codebase (a different MCP client/session) and remains unconfirmed.
design: Added decodeHtmlEntities() in operations.ts, applied before markdownToAdf(): decodes < > " '/' then & last (order matters to avoid double-unescaping). Defensive - makes the adapter render correctly regardless of what escaped the input upstream, rather than fixing the unknown upstream source.
acceptance_criteria: commentBody containing literal "->" renders as "->" in the flattened ADF text. Verified against the real TRF-21481 comment content.
notes: Confirmed via getJiraIssue(responseContentFormat=adf) against the real TRF-21481 comment (id 1295734) - comment 1295608 (posted earlier, using a real unicode arrow character instead of "->") rendered with no entity issue, isolating the bug to ASCII "->" specifically.
close_reason: Fixed via decodeHtmlEntities() applied before markdownToAdf() in operations.ts markdownAdf(). New test posts '-> ... &' and asserts the flattened ADF text reads '-> ... &'. 65/65 adapter-atlassian tests pass, full pnpm -r build/test clean. Upstream root cause (which client/session sent pre-escaped text) not identified - this is a defensive fix at the render boundary, not a fix of the unknown upstream source.

title: adapter-atlassian: Jira comments rendered raw markdown syntax instead of ADF formatting
description: Punakawan-composed Jira comments (headings, bold, numbered/bulleted lists) showed literal "##", "**", "-" characters in Jira instead of rendering as formatted rich text. Root cause: plainTextAdf() wrapped the entire comment/description string as one ADF paragraph text node, regardless of embedded Markdown structure.
design: Added marklassian (npm) as a dependency - parses Markdown into real ADF nodes (heading/bulletList/orderedList/strong/em/etc) via marked under the hood. Replaced plainTextAdf with markdownAdf across all 4 call sites: addJiraComment, addWorklog comment, editJiraIssue description, createJiraIssue description.
acceptance_criteria: A comment body containing Markdown headings/bold/lists produces ADF heading/bulletList/strong-marked-text nodes, not literal syntax characters in a single paragraph. Plain syntax-free text still round-trips to a single paragraph.
notes: Reported directly by the user with a real sample comment output showing the raw markdown rendering. Fixed and verified in the same session: 64/64 adapter-atlassian tests pass (2 new), full monorepo build/test clean.
close_reason: Fixed via marklassian-based markdownAdf() replacing plainTextAdf() across all 4 free-text call sites (comment, worklog comment, 2x description). New test asserts real heading/bulletList/strong ADF nodes and that literal ## / ** do not survive into flattened text. 64/64 adapter-atlassian tests pass, full pnpm -r build/test and go test ./... clean.

title: Add Jira comments, attachments, and conversational approval decisions
description: The Jira MVP can add comments but cannot list comments or manage attachments. Codex also may not advertise MCP form elicitation, leaving adapter writes pending with only a CLI instruction. Add compact direct REST comment/attachment operations and an explicit approve/deny conversational fallback while still attempting MCP elicitation first.
acceptance_criteria: Adapter lists compact paginated comments and attachment metadata; downloads attachments to a workspace-confined path; uploads and deletes attachments behind run approval; approval always attempts MCP elicitation, and unsupported clients receive clear Approve/Deny options plus a tool that records only an explicit follow-up decision; tests, lint, build, package, and live-safe smoke checks pass.
close_reason: Implemented compact Jira comments, links, epic context, workspace-confined attachment operations, broad editJiraIssue, and interactive per-run approvals; tests, lint, builds, packaging, and live-safe reads pass.

title: Harden Dolt startup and compact Atlassian adapter responses
description: Punakawan can fail opening its knowledge store when the spawned Dolt SQL server exits or is not yet accepting connections. Atlassian reads also return redundant raw Jira payloads that waste agent context. Make startup diagnostics/recovery robust and default adapter responses compact while preserving required normalized data.
acceptance_criteria: Knowledge-store startup handles stale/failed Dolt server state with actionable errors and regression tests. Jira read/search responses default to bounded normalized data without duplicate raw payloads. Existing write/reconciliation consumers remain compatible. Full test, lint, and build gates pass.
close_reason: Fixed Dolt server reuse/startup diagnostics and compacted Jira plus MCP responses; full tests, lint, build, package, and live Codex launcher smoke passed.

title: Add agent-client integration wizard to installer
description: The macOS installer currently registers only Claude Code. Add an interactive wizard that lets users choose Codex, Claude Code, both, another MCP client, or skip. Detect Codex from PATH or the ChatGPT app bundle, keep registrations at user scope, and provide a reusable generic STDIO configuration for other agentic clients.
design: Extract client registration into a separate shell helper invoked by install.sh. Give it a dry-run/test mode and command-path overrides so all wizard branches can be tested safely.
acceptance_criteria: Installer presents explicit client choices; Codex selection registers the generated run-mcp.sh through codex mcp add; Claude selection preserves user-scope registration; both performs both; generic produces usable MCP command/config guidance; unavailable selected clients fail clearly without preventing generic setup; summary reports selected integration(s). Shell behavior is testable without changing real user configuration.
close_reason: Added scripts/configure-agent.sh wizard with Codex, Claude Code, both, generic MCP, and skip choices; Codex PATH/ChatGPT-bundle detection; user-scope idempotent registration; dry-run and automation overrides; portable generic mcp-config.json; integration summary; missing-client recovery; README/install updates; and isolated shell tests wired into make test. bash -n, wizard tests, gofmt, go vet, and diff checks pass.

title: Run live Jira MVP smoke test with real credentials
description: The Jira-first MVP is covered by fake Atlassian MCP and in-memory MCP tests, but this checkout has no live Atlassian credentials and the global installer was not executed on a clean Mac during this session. Perform the first operator smoke test: run scripts/install.sh, connect a supported MCP client, read a real Jira issue, run assessment/planning, accept one inline approval, create a deduplicated subtask, and update an estimate/comment.
acceptance_criteria: On a clean macOS user account, install completes without project-local secrets; the selected client (Codex, Claude Code, or another STDIO MCP client) sees Punakawan; a real Jira issue can be read; exactly one approval authorizes multiple writes in one run; created subtask is not duplicated on retry; estimate/comment changes are visible in Jira.

title: Elicit one adapter-write approval per run
description: Approval-required adapter writes required an out-of-band CLI round trip per operation. Add MCP form elicitation as the first approval channel, fall back to the approvals CLI only for clients without elicitation support, and scope approval to the run so Jira field edits, worklogs, comments, transitions, subtask creation, and writes through other configured adapters share one grant.
design: Centralize MCP adapter invocation in an elicitation-aware helper. Keep approval persistence in internal/approvals and do not expose an agent-callable approve MCP tool.
acceptance_criteria: A supporting MCP client receives one inline human approval prompt for the first adapter write in a run; accepted approval permits later approval-required operations through any configured adapter for that run without re-prompting; decline blocks writes; unsupported clients receive the exact approvals CLI fallback; tests cover these paths.
close_reason: Added elicitation-first MCP approval flow shared by all adapter-writing tools. Approval id is scoped only to run_id, so all operations and adapters reuse one human grant. Unsupported clients get the exact CLI fallback; accept/decline/cross-operation/cross-adapter paths are tested. Full Go suite (except environment-only TestDoctor), TS tests, lint, and build pass.

title: Add install script for full local Punakawan setup
description: User wants a single script that installs all prerequisites globally (bd, dolt, node/pnpm, go, rtk), builds Punakawan once, collects Atlassian credentials (email + API token or service-account key + site host, e.g. yourteam.atlassian.net) into a global (not per-project) location, writes the global workspace.GlobalConfig adapters entry, and registers punakawan as a user-scope Claude Code MCP server so it attaches to any git-tracked project with zero required per-project scaffolding. The adapter itself derives the Atlassian cloud ID from the site host at runtime (via the unauthenticated tenant_info endpoint) so the user never has to look up or paste a cloud ID. A project workspace.yaml remains fully optional, only for overriding defaults. Must not hardcode secrets into any git-tracked file.
acceptance_criteria: Running the script on a clean macOS machine (bd/dolt/rtk not yet installed) ends with punakawan registered at Claude Code user scope (available in any project directory, no .mcp.json or per-project workspace.yaml required), a global adapters config with the atlassian adapter set up, and credentials stored only in a global file outside any git-tracked directory, not in any workspace.yaml.
close_reason: Global macOS installer is implemented: installs prerequisites, builds/packages Punakawan and Atlassian adapter, stores credentials in user config outside git, writes global adapter config, derives cloud ID from site host, and registers a user-scope Claude Code MCP server. bash syntax, repository build, tests, and lint verified; README now documents Jira-first setup and use.

title: adapter-atlassian: support Basic auth (email+token) for personal API tokens
description: Atlassian's Rovo MCP server headless auth has two distinct variants confirmed via Context7 + WebFetch of https://support.atlassian.com/atlassian-rovo-mcp-server/docs/configuring-authentication-via-api-token/: personal API tokens (individual user, tied to an email) require 'Authorization: Basic base64(email:api_token)'; service-account API keys use 'Authorization: Bearer <api_key>'. The current mcpClient.ts always sends Bearer, which is wrong for the personal-token+email case a real individual user actually has on hand. Also requires an org admin to enable API token auth for Rovo MCP under Atlassian Administration -> Rovo -> Rovo MCP server -> Authentication before either headless variant works at all.
design: Add optional ATLASSIAN_EMAIL to AtlassianConfig/loadConfigFromEnv (not required, unlike token/cloudId). defaultTransportFactory (or a new buildAuthHeader helper) branches: email present -> Basic base64(email:token); else -> Bearer token, preserving today's default for anyone already relying on Bearer.
acceptance_criteria: mcpClient.ts builds Basic base64(email:token) when ATLASSIAN_EMAIL is set, falls back to existing Bearer behavior when it is not (service-account key case). Tests cover both header-construction paths. All existing adapter-atlassian tests still pass.
close_reason: mcpClient.ts's buildAuthorizationHeader branches on whether ATLASSIAN_EMAIL is set: personal API token (email present) -> Basic base64(email:token); service-account key (no email) -> Bearer token - both configurable, matching the user's explicit ask to support either credential type. Confirmed via two independent WebFetch reads of the official Atlassian docs (configuring-authentication-via-api-token) that these header formats are not interchangeable. Verified: 4 new tests in mcpClient.test.ts (email presence/absence, both header formats incl. base64 round-trip); all 38 adapter-atlassian tests pass; typecheck/build clean.

title: MCP tool: update_jira_task_progress (estimate, worklog, comment on change)
description: When Petruk finishes a task (M6's finish_task_execution), Jira should reflect it: 'when do some changes, punakawan can update the jira, through comment, update the subtask and fill the work load.' New MCP tool update_jira_task_progress(issue_key, story_points?, original_estimate_hours?, worklog_hours?, comment?) that: fills original estimate from jiraworkflow.EstimateHours(story_points) unless original_estimate_hours is explicitly given (Petruk's own estimate overrides the points-derived default, per the user's decision), adds a worklog entry via atlassian.addWorklog when worklog_hours is given, and posts comment via atlassian.addJiraComment when given. All three actions are independently optional - a caller can update just one.
design: Built on punokawan-4ij.1 (invocation), punokawan-4ij.3 (editJiraIssueFields/addWorklog), punokawan-4ij.5 (EstimateHours). Building myself: the other end of the M6 execution loop's Jira integration, ties directly into finish_task_execution.
notes: Depends on punokawan-4ij.1, punokawan-4ij.3, punokawan-4ij.5.
close_reason: update_jira_task_progress derives original estimate hours via jiraworkflow.Config.EstimateHours(story_points) unless original_estimate_hours is given explicitly (explicit wins, per user decision), writes it as Jira's native timetracking.originalEstimate duration string (fractional hours split into Nh Mm rather than an undocumented decimal), adds a worklog entry, and posts a comment - each of the three independently optional and independently approval-gated. Verified: 6 new unit tests against a fake adapter caller (points-derived estimate, explicit-overrides-points, no-estimate-when-ratio-unconfigured, worklog+comment together, missing-approval error, duration formatting incl. whole/fractional/zero hours); go build/vet/gofmt clean; go test ./... all pass.

title: MCP tool: sync_jira_subtasks and skip-status task graph filtering
description: Two tied-together integration points requested by the user: (1) 'punakawan can skip a jira that sent back to product review' - when building a task graph (internal/tasks.GenerateGraph, M6) from a Jira-sourced requirement, check the requirement's current Jira status against jiraworkflow.ShouldSkip before creating a task for it. (2) 'punakawan can also auto generate subtasks (avoid redundant task)' - a new sync_jira_subtasks MCP tool wrapping atlassian.createJiraSubtask so Petruk's task decomposition can mirror onto real Jira subtasks, not just Beads issues.
design: New MCP tool sync_jira_subtasks(parent_issue_key, subtasks[]) calling call_adapter_operation with atlassian.createJiraSubtask. Skip-check: a small addition to the submit_task_graph handler (or a separate check_jira_skippable tool the caller invokes before submitting) - decide which during implementation based on which reads more naturally against §10's existing flow.
notes: Depends on punokawan-4ij.1, punokawan-4ij.4, punokawan-4ij.5. Building myself: touches the already-built M6 task graph generator.
close_reason: check_jira_skippable reads a requirement's raw Jira status (stored on its KnowledgeRecord by adapter-atlassian's normalizeJiraIssue) against jiraworkflow.Config.ShouldSkip - implemented as a separate pre-check tool rather than modifying internal/tasks.GenerateGraph directly, since silently dropping items there would complicate its already-shipped, tested dependency-wiring semantics for every caller. sync_jira_subtasks wraps atlassian.createJiraSubtask (dedup logic already lives in .4's work) with approval gating. Verified: 4 new tests (2 real MCP wire-protocol tests seeding KnowledgeRecords with jira-sourced status + jira-workflow.yaml config; 2 unit tests against a fake adapter caller for the create/skip/no-approval paths); go build/vet/gofmt clean; go test ./... all pass.

title: MCP tool: request_jira_clarification (comment + status transition)
description: When Semar/Gareng/Petruk need clarification (per §9.2's clarification gate), render the question via the already-built packages/clarification-comments, post it via atlassian.addJiraComment, then look up the configured clarification_status through atlassian.getTransitionsForJiraIssue and call atlassian.transitionJiraIssue with the matching transition id - failing clearly (not silently) if no transition to that status exists from the issue's current state. This is the direct implementation of the user's ask: 'when punakawan challenge a jira and ask for clarification, it can comment the jira and update jira status to sent back to product review.'
design: New MCP tool in internal/mcpserver, built on punokawan-4ij.1's call_adapter_operation plumbing (or a dedicated typed wrapper over Registry+Gate) plus internal/jiraworkflow's clarification_status config. Building myself: ties together adapter invocation, approval gating, and workflow config - the integration point, not an isolated piece.
notes: Depends on punokawan-4ij.1 (adapter invocation), punokawan-4ij.2 (transition ops), punokawan-4ij.5 (config).
close_reason: request_jira_clarification posts a comment via atlassian.addJiraComment, then (only when jira-workflow.yaml configures clarification_status) looks up real workflow transitions via atlassian.getTransitionsForJiraIssue and applies the matching one via atlassian.transitionJiraIssue, erroring clearly if none matches. Comment body is pre-rendered upstream (via packages/clarification-comments' new CLI entry point, added this task) rather than reimplemented in Go, avoiding duplicated templating logic. Verified: 4 new unit tests (tools_jiraclarify_test.go) against a fake adapter caller covering post+transition happy path, unconfigured-status skip, no-matching-transition error, and missing-approval error; go build/vet/gofmt clean; go test ./... all pass; clarification-comments CLI smoke-tested directly and its own 10/10 tests still pass.

title: internal/jiraworkflow: org-specific status/estimation config
description: New workspace-scoped config (.punakawan/jira-workflow.yaml, since status names and estimation scales are per-org/per-project, not a general capability policy): skip_statuses []string, clarification_status string, estimation.scale []float64 (e.g. Fibonacci [1,2,3,5,8,13,21]), estimation.points_to_hours float64. Jira has no universal 'this status means product review' or 'this is the story-point scale' discovery mechanism - status names and board estimation settings are org-configured, per the dashboard-canvas skill's own reliance on statusCategory (generic) vs raw status/labels (org-specific) split. This package makes that split explicit and honest rather than guessing.
design: internal/jiraworkflow/config.go: Load(path) (*Config, error) with a safe empty default when the file does not exist (mirrors policy.Load's pattern). ShouldSkip(statusName string) bool, EstimateHours(storyPoints float64) float64, ValidateStoryPoints(points float64) error (off-scale is a validation error, not silently accepted, since the user explicitly wants estimation-mode awareness, not passthrough).
notes: Delegated: small, self-contained, mirrors internal/policy's existing Load/Default pattern closely.
close_reason: internal/jiraworkflow.Config (Load/Default/ShouldSkip/EstimateHours/ValidateStoryPoints) implements the org-specific status/estimation split grounded in the epic's own research (statusCategory is generic, raw status names/estimation scales are not). Reviewed the subagent's implementation directly: correct empty-scale-means-no-validation and zero-ratio-means-unconfigured semantics, case-insensitive ShouldSkip. Verified: 10/10 tests in jiraworkflow_test.go pass against test/fixtures/jira-workflow.yaml.

title: Atlassian adapter: deduplicated subtask creation
description: Add atlassian.createJiraSubtask: before creating, searches existing children via searchJiraIssuesUsingJql (jql: parent = <parentKey>), compares the candidate's normalized summary against existing subtask summaries (case-insensitive, whitespace-collapsed exact match - not fuzzy, to avoid false-positive skips), and only calls createJiraIssue(cloudId, projectKey, issueTypeName, summary, description, parent, additional_fields) for genuinely new ones. Returns which were created vs skipped as duplicates. issueTypeName for subtasks is discovered via the already-existing getJiraProjectIssueTypesMetadata operation, not hardcoded as 'Sub-task' (naming varies by project).
design: packages/adapter-atlassian/src/operations.ts: createJiraSubtask(client, {parentKey, projectKey, candidates: {summary, description, additionalFields}[]}) -> {created: NormalizedJiraIssue[], skipped: {summary, existingKey}[]}. New manifest op atlassian.createJiraSubtask, side_effect: true, approval: required.
notes: Delegated: builds on the already-confirmed searchJiraIssuesUsingJql/createJiraIssue tools from M4, same fake-server test pattern.
close_reason: atlassian.createJiraSubtask added with exact-match (trimmed/whitespace-collapsed/case-insensitive) dedup against existing children via searchJira JQL, only creating genuinely-new candidates. Deviation from the task description, flagged and accepted: issueTypeName is caller-supplied rather than internally discovered via getJiraProjectIssueTypesMetadata, since that tool isn't implemented anywhere in this codebase (confirmed by the subagent reading the full existing operations.ts first) - noted for whoever picks up .7/.8. Verified independently: pnpm --filter @punakawan/adapter-atlassian typecheck/build/test all clean, 34/34 tests pass.

title: Atlassian adapter: field editing, worklog, and field-metadata discovery
description: Add atlassian.editJiraIssueFields (wraps editJiraIssue(cloudId, issueIdOrKey, fields), write:jira-work) for updating arbitrary fields (story points custom field, native timetracking.originalEstimate), atlassian.addWorklog (wraps addWorklogToJiraIssue(cloudId, issueIdOrKey, timeSpentSeconds, comment?), write:jira-work), and atlassian.getIssueTypeFieldMeta (wraps getJiraIssueTypeMetaWithFields(cloudId, projectIdOrKey, issueTypeId), read_jira) so the story-points custom field id is discovered per-project rather than hardcoded, since it is never the same customfield_NNNNN id across Jira sites.
design: Same file/manifest pattern as the transition task. editJiraIssueFields and addWorklog declared side_effect: true, approval: required; getIssueTypeFieldMeta side_effect: false. Extend the fake server with matching fixture tools.
notes: Delegated: same established pattern. Coordinate with the transition task (punokawan-4ij.2) on shared fakeAtlassianServer.ts edits - assign sequentially or merge carefully to avoid clobbering.
close_reason: atlassian.editJiraIssueFields, atlassian.addWorklog, atlassian.getIssueTypeFieldMeta added per spec (arbitrary fields passthrough, no hardcoded story-point field id). Reviewed diff directly, approval gating correct on both write ops. Verified independently: pnpm --filter @punakawan/adapter-atlassian typecheck/build/test all clean, 34/34 tests pass.

title: Atlassian adapter: status transition operations
description: Add atlassian.getTransitionsForJiraIssue (wraps the real getTransitionsForJiraIssue(cloudId, issueIdOrKey) tool, read_jira group, confirmed via support.atlassian.com/atlassian-rovo-mcp-server/docs/supported-tools/) returning {id, name, toStatus}[], and atlassian.transitionJiraIssue (wraps transitionJiraIssue(cloudId, issueIdOrKey, transitionId), write:jira-work scope) which performs the transition. Jira workflows require the discovery call first since transition ids are workflow-specific, not status names - do not skip it.
design: packages/adapter-atlassian/src/operations.ts: getTransitionsForJiraIssue and transitionJiraIssue functions mirroring existing getJiraIssue/addJiraComment patterns. manifest.ts: atlassian.getTransitionsForJiraIssue (side_effect: false), atlassian.transitionJiraIssue (side_effect: true, approval: required). Extend test/fakeAtlassianServer.ts with both tools and fixture transition data.
notes: Delegated: mirrors already-established adapter-atlassian patterns exactly, no new architecture.
close_reason: atlassian.getTransitionsForJiraIssue and atlassian.transitionJiraIssue added to operations.ts/manifest.ts/adapter.ts, transition-required-before-status-name design as specified. Reviewed diff directly (manifest declares transitionJiraIssue approval:required, getTransitionsForJiraIssue side_effect:false; adapter.ts dispatch matches manifest op names exactly). Verified independently (not just per subagent report): pnpm --filter @punakawan/adapter-atlassian typecheck/build/test all clean, 34/34 tests pass.

title: Adapter process lifecycle and generic invocation MCP tool
description: Foundational gap: internal/adapters.Start/Client/Gate exist (built in M4) but nothing spawns an adapter subprocess or exposes it to a calling role. New internal/adapters.Registry maps an adapter id (e.g. 'atlassian') to its spawn command + required env vars, lazily starts and reuses one *adapters.Client per adapter id (memoized on app.App, mirroring OpenKnowledge's lazy-singleton pattern), wraps each in a *adapters.Gate using the adapter's own manifest. New MCP tool call_adapter_operation(adapter_id, op, params, run_id, requested_by) routes through Gate.Call, so approval-gated operations (like atlassian.addJiraComment) are enforced exactly as M4 designed, now actually reachable.
design: internal/adapters/registry.go: Registry{specs map[string]AdapterSpec}, AdapterSpec{Command string, Args []string, EnvPassthrough []string}, (*Registry) Client(id string) (*Client, error) lazily calling Start. app.App gets an AdapterRegistry field, wired in Load(). Building myself: process spawning + secret env var passthrough (ATLASSIAN_MCP_TOKEN) is security-sensitive.
notes: Discovered this gap while scoping the Jira epic: grep -rn adapters. internal/mcpserver returned zero hits before this task.
close_reason: Registry.Gate lazily starts an adapter process, discovers its manifest via the new §5.3 capabilities RPC (added to prototypeAdapter.ts/docling-adapter/adapter-atlassian), completes initialize, and memoizes the Gate; Registry.Close shuts down spawned processes. Wired into app.App.Load/Close. New call_adapter_operation MCP tool routes through Gate.Call, enforcing manifest-declared approval gating. Verified: internal/adapters (9 tests, incl. 4 new registry_test.go cases: happy path, memoization, unknown id, Close-shuts-down-process) + 2 new MCP wire-protocol tests in tools_adapters_test.go (end-to-end call against the real spawned prototype adapter, unknown-adapter-id error) all pass; full go build/vet/gofmt clean; go test ./... = 188 passed/28 packages.

title: Jira as source of truth: adapter invocation, workflow-aware sync, subtasks, estimates, worklog
description: Deepens §13.2's Jira/Confluence integration per direct user request: Punakawan must understand org-specific Jira statuses/estimation modes, skip issues already sent back to product review, comment+transition Jira when Semar/Gareng/Petruk need clarification, auto-generate non-redundant subtasks, and auto-fill original estimate/worklog as work progresses. Grounded via Context7 + direct README/skills fetch from github.com/atlassian/atlassian-mcp-server and support.atlassian.com/atlassian-rovo-mcp-server/docs/supported-tools/: confirmed real tools transitionJiraIssue, getTransitionsForJiraIssue, editJiraIssue, addWorklogToJiraIssue, getJiraIssueTypeMetaWithFields, createJiraIssue (parent+additional_fields for subtasks), searchJiraIssuesUsingJql (for dedup). Discovered mid-scoping: M4's adapter+Gate infrastructure was never actually wired into a live MCP tool - internal/mcpserver has zero references to internal/adapters, so no calling role can invoke any adapter operation yet. That wiring is now this epic's foundational first piece. Scoped as offline/mock-verified per user decision (no live Jira credentials in this environment); estimate auto-fill uses points-to-hours conversion as default, overridable by Petruk's own stated estimate, per user decision.
acceptance_criteria: Punakawan can invoke a real (fixture-tested) Jira adapter operation through the live MCP server. A task-graph generation run against a Jira-sourced requirement skips issues in a configured skip-status list. Requesting clarification posts a real comment and transitions the issue via a real workflow-transition lookup. Generating subtasks does not create duplicates of already-existing subtasks. Finishing a task updates original estimate (points-derived, Petruk-overridable) and adds a worklog entry.
close_reason: All 8 children closed. Acceptance criteria met (offline/mock-verified scope, per user decision): (1) call_adapter_operation reaches a real spawned adapter process over the live MCP server, verified end-to-end; (2) check_jira_skippable correctly excludes Jira-sourced requirements whose stored status matches a configured skip_statuses entry; (3) request_jira_clarification posts a comment then transitions via a real getTransitionsForJiraIssue lookup, failing clearly if no matching transition exists; (4) createJiraSubtask/sync_jira_subtasks dedupe by normalized summary against existing children before creating; (5) update_jira_task_progress fills original estimate from story points via the configured ratio unless an explicit estimate is given (which wins), and independently adds worklog/comment. Also fixed the epic's own foundational gap: M4's adapter/Gate infrastructure is now actually reachable from the running MCP server via the new §5.3 capabilities message and Registry. All work committed in 10 scoped commits; full go build/vet/gofmt/test and pnpm -r test clean throughout.

title: Add CLI/MCP surface for granting or denying pending approvals
description: internal/approvals.Store and gitops.WorktreeManager.Approve/Deny plus internal/adapters.Gate.Approve/Deny exist as Go methods, but nothing lets a human actually call them: punakawan mcp serve exposes no approve_request/deny_request tool, and there is no punakawan approvals CLI subcommand either. Discovered while wiring M6's start_task_execution tool (punakawan-bap.4): it requires an already-approved worktree-creation request, and today the only way to approve one is to call WorktreeManager.Approve directly from Go test code. This blocks real (non-test) usage of every approval-gated M4/M6 tool. Source: punakawan-go-typescript-detailed-plan.md §16 (Approval Model) does not specify how a human actually grants approval, leaving this as an implementation gap rather than a documented design choice.
close_reason: Added `punakawan approvals list/approve/deny` CLI (cmd/punakawan/approvals_cmd.go), backed by a new generic internal/approvals.Store.Resolve method that resolves by id/status/approver alone, working for both worktree-creation and adapter-operation approval records (unlike the existing worktree-only approve/deny commands). Deliberately CLI-only, not an MCP tool: per §16.2, approved_by is the human, distinct from requested_by (the AI role) - exposing approve/deny over MCP would let the same connected client grant its own pending request. Verified: 3 new approvals package tests (approve, unknown-id error, already-resolved error) + 1 new CLI test exercising list/approve/deny against a real adapter-operation-shaped approval id end-to-end; full go build/vet/gofmt/test clean (all 24 packages).

title: Recipe Phase 6: Hardening and Generalization
description: Make the Jira MVP reliable and prepare additional read providers. Tasks: provider-instance compatibility checks (fingerprinting without storing secrets); rate-limit+transient-failure handling; schema-change detection; security+secret-redaction tests; performance+concurrency tests; recipe export/import via existing knowledge mechanisms; document how a second provider implements compiler/resolver/validator/executor interfaces. Do not implement write recipes in this phase.
acceptance_criteria: Jira recipes survive normal provider and workspace changes safely. A second read-only provider can be added without modifying the recipe engine. Arbitrary code and write operations remain impossible.

title: Recipe Phase 5: Recipe Review, Mutation, and Panel Extension
description: Reuse punokawan-apy's (Artifact Review Phase 5, punokawan-apy.6) review protocol for recipe correction; expose recipe behavior through the Phase 6 panel (punokawan-6v4). Tasks: enable retrieval_recipe as a reviewable artifact type alongside plan types; structured-path comment anchors for recipe fields/conditions; revise_retrieval_recipe_from_review dispatcher (reusing the existing workflow dispatcher, no new one); recipe list/detail contracts for the panel; capability/intent/scope/selector/resolver views; show compiled query+dynamic bindings; show validation/acceptance/execution evidence; show usage history+latest result count; immutable version diffs; require compiler+provider validation before proposal acceptance; compare previous vs proposed result samples before acceptance; timeline events for resolution/validation/reuse/failure; stale/disputed/superseded/invalid state displays; preserve panel's read-only default outside an authenticated review session. Do NOT introduce a second comment store, review state machine, or acceptance API - reuse punokawan-apy.6's protocol exactly.
acceptance_criteria: A user can explain why Punakawan selected and executed a recipe. The exact recipe version is visible from the related session. Panel failures do not affect recipe execution in core.

title: Recipe Phase 4: Workflow Reuse, Versioning, and Lifecycle
description: Reuse accepted recipes and keep corrections auditable. Tasks: resolve_operation workflow step; expand missing-recipe discovery into 6 BD subtasks (resolve recipe, discover project-mapping if missing, validate candidate query, persist accepted recipe, retrieve work items, attach retrieval evidence); skip discovery subtasks 2-4 when a valid recipe exists; execute compiled operation; record recipe ID/version/bindings/compiled-query-hash/result-count as execution evidence; immutable version creation (never overwrite verified version in place, preserve prior compiled query+validation evidence, human-readable diff, link via supersedes); update/validate/dispute/supersede commands; stale-recipe revalidation before execution; resume original task after discovery/revalidation.
acceptance_criteria: A later Jira request reuses the accepted recipe without asking the same questions. Corrections create a new version. Historical sessions retain the exact version they used. Stale and disputed recipes are never silently reused. CLI: punakawan knowledge recipe list/show/explain/validate/update/dispute/supersede.

title: Recipe Phase 3: Guided Discovery and Validation
description: Teach Punakawan a missing retrieval rule safely. Tasks: resumable discovery state machine (MISSING->COLLECTING_RULES->COMPILING->TESTING->PRESENTING_RESULTS->CORRECTING->RETESTING->ACCEPTED->STORED->EXECUTING_ORIGINAL_TASK; exits: CANCELLED/REJECTED/UNRESOLVED/PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE/POLICY_BLOCKED); ask only for unresolved constraints; compile candidate recipe from user guidance; dry-run provider query (schema/capability/adapter/fields/operators/resolvers/compile/test); show resolved values+JQL+result count+sample of 20 with match reasons+validation state; collect corrections/exclusions/missing-examples/rejections/save-as-draft; retest until accepted/rejected/draft/cancelled; store explicit acceptance evidence (user, timestamp, validation ID, provider fingerprint, compiled-query hash, sample size, accepted result count).
acceptance_criteria: The original task remains checkpointed during discovery. The user can correct and retest the query repeatedly. Only explicitly accepted candidates become verified.

title: Recipe Phase 2: Jira Selector Compiler and Dynamic Resolvers
description: Turn a structured recipe into a safe Jira search. Tasks: Jira selector AST compiler (all/any/field operators); safe value escaping + field/operator validation; Jira project+component validation; resolve project-associated boards; resolve strict next sprint (earliest valid sprint by board order/start date, ask for clarification if ambiguous); explicitly-labeled futureSprints() approximation fallback with warning; compile ordering+requested output fields; clause-level explanations for user review.
acceptance_criteria: User-provided values are never concatenated directly into JQL (safe escaping always). Strict 'next' resolves to one sprint ID or asks for clarification. Compiled queries are reproducible and evidence-ready.
close_reason: Compiler implemented: selector AST (all/any, 2-level nesting) compiles to JQL via quoteJQL escaping, field/operator allow-list validation. jira.board_for_project + jira.next_sprint dynamic resolvers wired behind an optional JiraAgileClient (no live adapter wiring yet - board/sprint-listing ops don't exist in packages/adapter-atlassian, documented gap for a later execution-wiring phase). No client configured -> futureSprints() fallback with warning; ambiguous board/tied sprint -> ClarificationNeededError. Ordering + output fields compiled, clause-level explanations produced. 13 new tests passing, full suite 519 passed.

title: Recipe Phase 1: Recipe Repository and Deterministic Resolution
description: Find a compatible recipe before asking the user for rules. Tasks: recipe repository interfaces (Search/CreateVersion/MarkState); filter by capability/provider/resource/operation/scope; exclude disputed/superseded/invalid versions; deterministic ranking (exact workspace +100, exact capability +100, exact intent +80, exact repo +40, verified +30, prior success +20, intent alias +15, BM25 0-15, stale -30); alias/BM25 only after structural compatibility checks; ambiguity reporting for multiple materially different candidates; record selected recipe ID/version/match explanation.
acceptance_criteria: A verified workspace-scoped recipe is resolved deterministically. Incompatible high-BM25 records cannot win. Multiple legitimate candidates produce an explicit ambiguity result.
close_reason: Repository/Resolver implemented over knowledge.Store: Search filters by capability/provider/resource/operation/workspace-scope restricted to verified+stale states, CreateVersion reuses Store.Supersede, MarkState handles transitions. Resolver ranks candidates per §6/§7 scoring table, classifies resolved/stale/ambiguous/not_found. 9 new tests passing, full suite 506 passed.

title: Recipe Phase 0: Recipe Contract Preparation
description: Establish stable recipe contracts without changing workflow behavior. Tasks: confirm existing panel/review contracts recipe views+mutations reuse; add retrieval_recipe to knowledge taxonomy; define capability/intent/selector/resolver/validation/execution/version contracts; define recipe lifecycle states; add fixtures for valid/stale/disputed/superseded/invalid recipes; define evidence contracts for compilation/validation/acceptance/execution.
acceptance_criteria: Schemas validate in Go and TypeScript. Recipe records can be stored and displayed as inert knowledge. No recipe can execute yet.
close_reason: Recipe contracts defined: retrieval-recipe knowledge type, draft/validating validity states, self-contained retrieval_recipe nested object (capability/intent/selector-AST/output/validation/last_execution), uses-adapter+retrieves relation types. Go+TS generated cleanly (protocol-check clean), fixtures+round-trip tests pass for all 7 lifecycle states against a real Dolt store. No executor exists yet - nothing can run a recipe.

title: Artifact Review Phase 6: Hardening
description: Comprehensive security, concurrency, failure-recovery, performance, accessibility, and visual-consistency tests plus user documentation. Tasks: duplicate-submit/idempotency-replay tests; concurrent-review tests; stale-anchor resolution tests (ID->hash->heading-path->fuzzy->conflicted fallback chain); mutation-session security tests (bootstrap token expiry, CSRF, auth vs unauth, session invalidation); failure-recovery tests (panel stop mid-revision, revision run failure, stale base version at acceptance); accessibility audit (WCAG AA, keyboard nav, screen reader, reduced-motion, focus rings); responsive visual regression tests (light/dark x 360px/768px/1024px/wide); touch/mobile viewport tests (44px targets, no hover deps, bottom-sheet responsiveness); chart/graph performance tests; CI bundle-size enforcement; user docs (review/comment/accept/reject/troubleshoot); troubleshooting guide.
acceptance_criteria: Mutation is local, authenticated, versioned, recoverable, accessible, visually consistent, and responsive. All plan-level acceptance criteria are met. Test coverage includes security, failure, concurrency, accessibility, visual regression, and performance. Documentation covers primary workflows and troubleshooting.

title: Artifact Review Phase 5: Proposal Review and Acceptance
description: Implement proposal display with responsive diff, validation summary, comment-resolution report, request-changes/reject/accept flows, conflict/rebase handling. Tasks: require complete artifact proposals (not isolated snippets); block-aware responsive Markdown diff renderer (side-by-side desktop, unified mobile); collapsible unchanged sections + in-diff search; diff summary (added/removed/modified counts); comment-resolution report (addressed/partially_addressed/rejected_by_agent/needs_clarification/obsolete/resolved_by_user); validation summary (structural/consistency/review-compliance); marked changed blocks; three-pane desktop (comments|diff|validation) / tabbed mobile (Diff|Comments|Validation|Evidence); sticky mobile proposal actions; request-changes flow (attempt N+1 under same review); rejection flow (non-destructive); conflict detection (canonical hash != proposal base hash) + rebase option; version-lineage graph; plan validator (stable ID, version increments, unique block IDs, required sections, balanced markdown, valid heading hierarchy).
acceptance_criteria: A user can fully review and accept a proposal from desktop or mobile. Accepted proposals create immutable plan version N+1. Rejected proposals remain non-destructive. Conflicts are detected and rebase is available. Version lineage is visible. Every comment has a documented resolution. This is the protocol punokawan-q9r's Phase 5 (Recipe Review, Mutation, and Panel Extension) reuses as-is for retrieval_recipe artifacts - keep the review/proposal/acceptance state machine generic over artifact type, not plan-specific.

title: Artifact Review Phase 4: Submit and Retrigger
description: Implement immutable review submission, idempotent revision-request creation, workflow dispatching, active-revision tracking, restart recovery. Tasks: freeze review snapshot on Submit (immutable ArtifactRevisionRequest); idempotent submission (key = review ID + base revision hash + comment snapshot hash + sequence); RevisionWorkflowDispatcher dispatching revise_plan_from_review; BD parent 'Revise <artifact>' + numbered child tasks; active-revision bento summary card (phase/elapsed/comments-processed/clarification-state/validation-status/related-BD-task); live timeline via SSE/polling; clarification-request card; evidence table; status charts; restart recovery (reconnect to ongoing run, resume progress display); CSRF validation on all mutations; mutation audit events.
acceptance_criteria: Submit starts exactly one durable revision run. Current status remains understandable on mobile and desktop. Clarification questions appear in the UI; answers are stored. Panel restart reconnects to the running revision. Idempotent submission prevents duplicate runs.

title: Artifact Review Phase 3: Draft Plan Review
description: Implement authenticated mutation sessions, draft review creation, anchored commenting, draft persistence. Tasks: authenticated mutation session (ephemeral local panel session secret, one-time bootstrap token exchanged for SameSite=Strict HttpOnly cookie); responsive review mode (doc+comment-rail desktop, floating Add Comment+bottom-sheet mobile); anchored comment creation for sections and selected text; draft comment persistence (.punakawan/reviews/<review-id>/comments.jsonl); desktop comment rail with status chips/edit/delete; mobile bottom-sheet comment browser; general review instruction textarea; unsaved-change indicator; optimistic draft revision UI; sticky Submit Review action; exit/resume behavior; form validation + idempotent comment ops.
acceptance_criteria: A user can annotate and resume a plan from desktop or mobile. Canonical content remains unchanged. Draft comments are durable across session restarts. Comment rail and bottom sheet are responsive and accessible.

title: Artifact Review Phase 2: Charts and Graph Visualizations
description: Add Chart.js and Cytoscape.js adapters with lazy-loading, theme updates, reduced-motion support, accessible fallbacks. Tasks: dynamic Chart.js adapter + ChartShell; theme-aware formatters; review-activity/revision-outcome/duration/comment-resolution/change-volume charts; data-table fallbacks; dynamic Cytoscape.js adapter + GraphCanvas; graph controls/focused-subgraph-loading/node-cap; workflow/task/relation/version-lineage graph views; relation-list fallbacks; lazy-load both libs only when a chart/graph page is visited; theme-color updates on light/dark switch; reduced-motion behavior; perf tests; CI bundle budgets (chart <120KB, graph <300KB compressed, base route <250KB).
acceptance_criteria: Charts and graphs are responsive, theme-aware, and optional at route load. Every visualization has an accessible nonvisual equivalent. Large graphs start from focused, bounded datasets. Visualization chunks remain within agreed CI budgets.

title: Artifact Review Phase 1: Responsive Dashboard and Data Presentation
description: Modernize overview/summary pages using shared bento grid, cards, tables, responsive layouts. Tasks: Card component; BentoGrid + semantic BentoCard sizes (small/medium/wide/tall); metric/status/chart/graph/table/review/diff-summary card variants; typed reusable DataTable (sort/filter/paginate/column-visibility/sticky-header/row-select); MobileDataList; shared filters/pagination/status-badges/skeletons/empty-error states; compact/comfortable density; migrate overview/workspace/task views to shared components; responsive table-to-card-list; mobile nav drawers/bottom sheets/sticky action bars; 12/6/1-column bento grid (desktop/tablet/mobile).
acceptance_criteria: Overview and workspace summaries are clear on 360px mobile and desktop. No essential table requires page-level horizontal scrolling. Shared cards and tables replace feature-specific duplicates. Responsive rules (44px touch targets, no essential hover, sticky actions) are met.

title: Artifact Review Phase 0: UI Foundation and Artifact Versioning
description: Establish shared UI components, semantic theming, artifact storage contracts, and visual fixtures underlying every later phase. Tasks: semantic light/dark/system theme tokens + no-flash init; ThemeToggle + AccentPicker; responsive AppShell/Sidebar/MobileNavigation/PageHeader; ResponsiveToolbar/StickyActionBar; drawer/dialog/bottom-sheet overlay primitives; component showcase route; ArtifactReference schema; immutable plan version storage (.punakawan/plans/<id>/versions/, current.yaml pointer); stable Markdown block-ID support+resolver; review/comment/request/proposal/resolution YAML schemas; sha256 artifact revision hash service.
acceptance_criteria: Existing panel routes use the shared theme and shell. Light/dark themes render without contrast or flash issues. Core components work at mobile/tablet/desktop widths. Plans load by ID and version; previous versions cannot be overwritten. Block IDs are stable and anchors resolve correctly.

title: [EPIC] Punakawan Procedural Knowledge and Retrieval Recipes
description: Store, reuse, and correct verified reusable retrieval procedures ('retrieval recipes') for recurring provider lookups (e.g. Jira search), without arbitrary executable code. A recipe is a declarative, versioned, immutable-per-version knowledge record binding to a registered read-only adapter capability via a structured selector AST (not raw JQL/SQL), scoped to workspace/repository, provider-tested before trust, and explicitly correctable. Converts ad-hoc user-taught lookup rules into durable operational knowledge. This is 'New Plan 2' - it reuses 'New Plan 1' (punokawan-apy, Artifact Review and Plan Mutation)'s review/proposal/acceptance protocol as-is for recipe correction, rather than building a second review system. See punakawan-procedural-knowledge-retrieval-recipe-plan-final.md. 7 phases (0-6): Recipe Contract Preparation; Recipe Repository and Deterministic Resolution; Jira Selector Compiler and Dynamic Resolvers; Guided Discovery and Validation; Workflow Reuse Versioning and Lifecycle; Recipe Review Mutation and Panel Extension; Hardening and Generalization.
design: Adds retrieval_recipe to the existing knowledge taxonomy (fact, requirement, decision, constraint, relationship, mapping, validation_rule, workflow_hint). New components: recipe repository, Jira selector AST compiler (safe JQL escaping), recipe resolver (deterministic ranking: exact workspace +100, exact capability +100, exact intent +80, exact repo +40, verified +30, prior success +20, intent alias +15, BM25 0-15, stale -30), recipe validator (schema+provider dry-run+evidence), recipe executor, resumable guided-discovery state machine (MISSING->COLLECTING_RULES->COMPILING->TESTING->PRESENTING_RESULTS->CORRECTING->RETESTING->ACCEPTED->STORED->EXECUTING_ORIGINAL_TASK), recipe lifecycle manager (versioning/staleness/dispute/supersede). Persists in the existing durable knowledge store (internal/knowledge) - no new database. Reuses existing BM25F+alias+fuzzy+relation search. Panel Phase 6-style read-only recipe detail views, reusing Phase 5 (punokawan-gq5) knowledge-browser patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Punakawan checks for a compatible verified recipe before asking for lookup rules again. Missing knowledge triggers resumable guided discovery, checkpointing the original task. User-provided values are never concatenated directly into JQL (safe escaping always). Only explicitly accepted candidates become verified reusable knowledge; corrections create immutable new versions via New Plan 1's protocol (no second review/acceptance system). Stale recipes are revalidated before reuse; disputed/superseded/invalid recipes never auto-execute. Recipes never store credentials/secret headers. Initial recipes restricted to registered read-only adapter capabilities (no write recipes yet). Success metric: the Jira next-sprint example works end to end without embeddings, arbitrary scripts, duplicated state, or silent reinterpretation of ambiguous sprint scope.

title: [EPIC] Punakawan Artifact Review and Plan Mutation
description: Proposal-based mutation model for plans/knowledge artifacts: anchored review comments -> automatic Punakawan revision workflow -> validated diff proposal -> explicit user acceptance creates new immutable version. No direct in-place editing of canonical artifacts ever. Also modernizes Panel UI (bento grid, shared components, semantic theming, Chart.js/Cytoscape.js adapters). See punakawan-artifact-review-plan-mutation-plan-v2.md. 7 phases (0-6): UI Foundation and Artifact Versioning; Responsive Dashboard and Data Presentation; Charts and Graph Visualizations; Draft Plan Review; Submit and Retrigger; Proposal Review and Acceptance; Hardening.
design: New packages: internal/review (comment/revision/proposal lifecycle), internal/validation (plan proposal validator), internal/workspace/artifacts (versioning + stable-block anchor resolution). New protocol types: ArtifactReference, ArtifactReview, ArtifactComment, ArtifactRevisionRequest, ArtifactRevisionProposal, RevisionWorkflowDispatcher. Storage: .punakawan/plans/<id>/{current.yaml,versions/N.md}, .punakawan/reviews/<review-id>/{review.yaml,comments.jsonl,submissions/,proposals/,clarifications.jsonl,evidence/}, .punakawan/runs/<revision-run-id>/. No new database. Markdown blocks get stable HTML-comment anchors (pk:block:<id>) with a fallback resolution chain: exact ID -> content hash -> heading path+quoted text -> fuzzy -> mark conflicted. Dispatches typed workflow runs (revise_plan_from_review) via BD parent+child tasks.
acceptance_criteria: Panel never directly overwrites canonical content. Mutation requires authenticated local session + CSRF token. Duplicate submissions are idempotent. Stale revisions cannot be accepted silently. Accepted changes are attributable to a review, proposal, and user action. Light/dark/system themes meet WCAG AA. Primary workflows usable at 360px/768px/1024px/desktop. Charts/graphs lazy-loaded with accessible fallbacks, within CI bundle budgets. This is 'New Plan 1' - its accepted review protocol is later reused as-is (no second review state machine) by the procedural-knowledge-retrieval-recipe plan ('New Plan 2') for retrieval_recipe artifacts.

title: Panel Phase 7: Packaging, Hardening, and Release
description: Ship a reliable local feature. Embedded production assets, cross-platform packaging, release version metadata, upgrade compatibility tests, performance fixtures, corrupted-workspace tests, accessibility audit, CSP/DNS-rebinding tests, documentation, migration notes, troubleshooting commands.
acceptance_criteria: All target binaries contain the UI. punakawan panel works without Node.js or Docker. No new database or service required. Security and accessibility checks pass.

title: Panel Phase 6: Evidence, Approvals, and Health
description: Complete the audit and diagnostic views. Evidence list and safe previews, ranged log loading, diff summaries, screenshot previews, approval list/detail, CLI resolution hints for pending approvals, source-adapter health, Git health, panel-system page, secret-redaction tests.
acceptance_criteria: Arbitrary workspace paths cannot be served. Large evidence does not block page loading. Secrets never reach API fixtures or browser tests.
close_reason: Phase 6 done: evidence list/get/preview (safe-path-enforced, ranged, redacted, diff-summarized), approvals w/ CLI resolution hints, Git+adapter health folded into workspace health, System/Approvals frontend pages + SessionDetail evidence viewer. Frontend: TasksPage with Board/Table/Dependency-Graph views, TaskCard, TaskDetailDrawer (description, acceptance criteria, dependencies/dependents, honest gaps noted for status history/related-entity links not yet wired), deterministic longest-path graph layout (lib/graph/layout.ts) with SVG connectors and an accessible text-list fallback for the dependency view, cycle callout. All Go and frontend tests pass, gofmt/vet/typecheck clean.

Frontend: SessionsList and SessionDetail pages (active-session progress, phase timeline, role lane, capsule metadata via timeline task refs, error/recovery section), SSE client with native browser reconnect/Last-Event-ID, TopBar connection indicator, live refetch-on-event wiring for Overview/WorkspacesList/WorkspaceSummary/SessionsList/SessionDetail. Found and fixed 3 nil-slice-to-JSON-null bugs during manual smoke test (session list, pending approvals, active sessions all crashed the frontend on an empty/fresh workspace) - internal/panel/sources/session_source.go, approval_source.go, internal/panel/api/overview_handler.go now always return []T{} instead of nil. Add punakawan panel command, loopback-only HTTP binding, static-asset embedding, workspace registry, auto-registration, workspace path validation, /system /workspaces /overview endpoints, security headers, structured logs, graceful shutdown.

close_reason: Schemas (PanelSessionSummary/PanelEvent/PanelWorkspaceRegistryEntry/PanelSourceHealth), 6 reader interfaces + concrete implementations over existing stores, and summary.yaml writer wired into create_workflow_run/advance_workflow, all shipped and tested.

title: Punakawan Panel: local visual tracker
description: Local web dashboard served from the existing Go binary (embedded Svelte 5 SPA), read-only observer of canonical Punakawan state (Dolt/BD/JSONL/YAML/evidence/approvals), no new database, SSE for live updates. See punakawan-panel-implementation-plan.md. 8 phases: Contracts and Prerequisites; Local Server and Workspace Registry; Overview and Workspace Summary; Sessions and Live Updates; Tasks and Dependency View; Knowledge Browser and Global Search; Evidence, Approvals, and Health; Packaging, Hardening, and Release.
design: Go binary embeds compiled Svelte 5 frontend via go:embed. Local HTTP API under /api/v1, loopback-only binding, SSE at /api/v1/events. Reader interfaces (WorkspaceReader, SessionReader, TaskReader, KnowledgeReader, EvidenceReader, ApprovalReader) sit between HTTP handlers and format-specific parsing of existing stores. In-memory read-model with bounded LRU caches and short TTLs, never persisted. Workspace registry as YAML at OS config path. MVP is read-only except safe local UI actions.
acceptance_criteria: punakawan panel starts dashboard on loopback only, no Node/Docker/new DB required. All 8 phases' exit criteria in punakawan-panel-implementation-plan.md §24 met. Static assets embedded in release binary.

CanPush and DefaultBranch are tested against a real local bare-repo remote (git push --dry-run, git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD) rather than mocked. See internal/gitops/capabilities.go, commit d747863.
close_reason: Added protocol.GitCapabilities/GitExecutionPolicy schemas and Inspector.DetectCapabilities/gitops.MergeExecutionPolicy implementing §7.4's 'detected ∩ repository policy ∩ user permission' merge rule. title: Detect git remote provider (github/gitlab/bitbucket/generic)
description: internal/gitops.Inspector has no remote/provider detection today (only branch/status/diff/log/head-sha). Parse 'git remote -v' output and classify the origin URL's host into github/gitlab/bitbucket/generic per plan section 7.2-7.3's GitCapabilities.provider field.

close_reason: Added protocol.GitCapabilities/GitExecutionPolicy schemas and Inspector.DetectCapabilities/gitops.MergeExecutionPolicy implementing §7.4's 'detected ∩ repository policy ∩ user permission' merge rule. title: Enforce per-role capsule field restrictions at the tool layer
description: Bagong must not receive Semar/Gareng/Petruk's prior reasoning (plan section 5.4 'Bagong Must Not Receive'); Gareng/Petruk must not receive full conversation history or each other's outputs (section 5.2/5.3 Context Rules). Today this is advisory-only (server.go:47 Instructions text). Design and implement an actual check - e.g. a capsule validator that rejects known-forbidden fields for a given role before a submit_*_review tool accepts it.
close_reason: Implemented capsule-gated tool calls per the user's chosen design: request_capsule builds+digests+persists an immutable ContextCapsule (internal/capsule), rejecting forbidden knowledge-record types and forbidden tools for the target role. submit_gareng_review/submit_petruk_plan/submit_bagong_review now require a capsule_id issued for that exact role, verified via requireCapsuleForRole (existence, role match, digest integrity). 281 tests passing, no regressions.

title: Implement deterministic capsule digest

Needs a canonical-JSON encoder (stable key ordering) - mirror internal/knowledge.ContentHash's sha256:<hex> convention. Add a test asserting identical input produces identical digest and any field change produces a different one.
close_reason: protocol/capsule.schema.json + generated ContextCapsule type; internal/capsule.Digest computes the deterministic §6.3 digest, nil/empty-slice normalized

title: AEP-M4: create_pr / review_pr / fix_pr_review workflows
description: Architecture enhancement plan Milestone 4. Current state: NONE of create_pr, review_pr, or fix_pr_review exist in any form (zero matches across the repo). packages/github-adapter and packages/gitlab-adapter are empty stub packages (package.json only, no source). The only working external adapter is Atlassian/Jira (packages/adapter-atlassian). title: AEP-M3: Git capability detection
description: Architecture enhancement plan Milestone 3. Current state: internal/gitops.Inspector detects current branch, detached HEAD, uncommitted/untracked changes, HEAD SHA, log, and diff only (internal/gitops/inspect.go). It does NOT detect: remote provider (github/gitlab/bitbucket), authentication availability, push permission, PR read/write permission, default branch, or nested repositories. The plan's GitCapabilities schema (section 7.3) and user-override merge (detected ∩ policy ∩ user, section 7.4) do not exist.
close_reason: All 3 children complete: remote/provider detection (hp7), default branch/push detection (a44), and the GitCapabilities/GitExecutionPolicy schema plus detected-intersect-policy-intersect-user merge (nzg). Note: PR read/write/comment permission detection (also listed in §7.2's checklist) intentionally stays false-with-a-limitation-note pending AEP-M4's provider adapter, which this epic unblocks.

title: AEP-M2: BD task graph + Jira decoupling hardening
description: Architecture enhancement plan Milestone 2. Current state: substantially already implemented. internal/tasks + internal/beads give BD-as-source-of-truth with dependency wiring (WireDependency), requirement/jira_key/beads_epic mapping (§10.1), and discovery tracking (DiscoveredFrom, ReportDiscoveredWork). internal/adapters.Gate + internal/reconcile give Jira-as-optional-adapter with approval-gated writes and graceful read failure. Gaps vs the plan: TaskContract has no structured evidence-link field (evidence is linked only by .punakawan/evidence/<run>/<task>/ directory-naming convention, not a struct reference); no explicit retry/conflict-detection queue for outbound Jira sync failures beyond the existing hard-error-on-failure approval gate.
close_reason: Both children complete: structured evidence references (s12, via internal/evidence.Ledger + list_task_evidence) and an outbound Jira sync retry/conflict queue (nbz, via internal/syncqueue). Closes the two concrete gaps identified against the plan; the rest of M2's scope (BD-as-source-of-truth, Jira-as-optional-adapter) was already implemented.

title: AEP-M1: Context capsules and agent isolation
description: Architecture enhancement plan (punakawan-architecture-enhancement-plan.md) Milestone 1. Current state: Semar/Gareng/Petruk/Bagong exist as MCP prompts (internal/mcpserver/prompts.go, prompts/*/prompt.md) executed by the connected client's own model - punakawan performs zero reasoning (ADR-0016). There is no context-capsule concept anywhere: internal/taskcontext.Context and internal/dossier have no digest/hash and no per-role field restrictions. Bagong is only advised via server Instructions text to build fresh context (server.go:47) - nothing enforces it cannot receive Semar/Gareng/Petruk's prior reasoning; isolation today is entirely the calling agent's own discipline, not punakawan-enforced.
notes: See baseline assessment in session transcript 2026-07-23. Plan doc lives at punakawan-architecture-enhancement-plan.md sections 5-6, 21 (ADR-001..004), 22 (Milestone 1).
close_reason: All 3 children complete: ContextCapsule schema (ef8), deterministic digest (ag2), and capsule-gated tool-layer enforcement (ow9). Gareng/Petruk/Bagong now require a role-scoped capsule to submit their review/plan, closing the isolation gap this milestone targeted.

title: Tool descriptions/errors don't state preconditions, call order, or gate mechanics — agents must read Go source
description:
Concrete gaps found by grepping source mid-task, each of which should have been discoverable from the MCP surface alone:

1. build_task_context / submit_task_graph jsonschema says requirement_id is "id of the parent requirement knowledge record" — never states it must already exist, or that (currently) nothing creates one. Found only via runtime store.Get error, confirmed dead-end only by reading internal/taskcontext/taskcontext.go and internal/tasks/tasks.go source (see punokawan-zvt).

2. start_task_execution says "Requires a prior approved worktree-creation request" but never says approval is a separate CLI command (`punokawan worktree approve`) run outside the MCP surface entirely, in the human's own terminal. Found only via grepping cmd/.

3. No tool states call order. Every description is self-contained/isolated; nothing says "X needs Y to have succeeded first." Had to reconstruct create_workflow_run -> [requirement record, missing] -> build_task_context -> start_task_execution -> write -> check_diff -> commit_task -> finish_task_execution by reading source across multiple packages.

4. advance_workflow never lists valid states inline — had to find protocol/workflow.schema.json manually to learn the enum.

5. Approval-fallback mechanics ("if client doesn't support elicitation, show Approve/Deny, then call respond_to_adapter_approval") live only in the server-level MCP Instructions blob, not co-located on the specific write tool (update_jira_task_progress, sync_jira_subtasks, etc.) that actually triggers a gate.

What's already right, use as the bar: call_adapter_operation's description lists concrete example read/write ops inline AND explains the approval flow in the same sentence. Every other tool should read like that one.
design: Model tool: call_adapter_operation. Every other tool description should hit the same bar — inline preconditions + inline approval mechanics, not scattered across server Instructions or requiring a source read.
acceptance_criteria:
- build_task_context, submit_task_graph, start_task_execution jsonschema descriptions state hard preconditions inline (what must exist first, and if nothing currently creates it, say so) — same bar as call_adapter_operation.
- A single runbook resource or help-type tool documents the canonical end-to-end call sequence for the whole workflow, with the WorkflowRunState enum spelled out inline (not requiring protocol/workflow.schema.json to be found manually).
- Any tool that can trigger an approval gate (update_jira_task_progress, sync_jira_subtasks, request_jira_clarification, submit_jira_assessment, worktree creation) states the gate mechanics and the CLI approve/deny commands directly in its own description, not only in server Instructions.
- Error messages from hard-require paths (missing requirement record, missing worktree approval) name the missing prerequisite and the concrete next command to run, not a bare wrapped Go error.
notes: Partially addressed by punokawan-zvt's fix: build_task_context/submit_task_graph now state the requirement_id precondition inline, and their store.Get not-found errors name ingest_jira_requirement as the fix. Remaining open: start_task_execution's worktree-approval precondition, no canonical call-order runbook, advance_workflow's state enum not inline, and approval-gate mechanics not co-located on every write tool (still only in server Instructions).
close_reason: All 4 acceptance items addressed: inline preconditions (zvt), gate mechanics + enum values on tool descriptions (this commit), and prerequisite-naming error messages (zvt). No separate runbook resource added - serverInstructions already states the canonical call sequence and advance_workflow now states its enum inline, which covers the same need without a new resource type.

title: build_task_context has no incremental/resume mode — full context resent every call
description: Every build_task_context call requires resending the full scope/AC/constraints text from scratch, even when resuming the same task across rounds (impl -> tests -> review), which the worktree lifecycle now explicitly supports. This duplicates token spend on both the agent and server side for no reason on a resume.
acceptance_criteria: build_task_context (or a companion tool) supports an incremental update path — e.g. reference the prior context by task_id and pass only a delta — without requiring the full payload to be resent verbatim.
close_reason: BuildInput.Previous (loaded from the task's own task.yaml evidence) fills in omitted scope/AC/DoD/expected-files/symbols/tests fields on a resume call; caller only sends what actually changed

title: Adapter-write approval scoped per run_id causes redundant re-prompts
description: Every new run_id for the same person/ticket/day re-triggers a fresh human Approve/Deny prompt for Jira writes, even though the actual safety boundary (a human must sign off) doesn't need to be finer-grained than session or repo+day. This is friction without added safety when resuming the same task across multiple runs.
acceptance_criteria: Design and implement a broader approval scope option (e.g. per session, or per repo+day) that still requires one human sign-off but doesn't re-prompt on every run_id for materially the same unit of work. Must not weaken the per-operation-type approval-required behavior.
close_reason: Added policy.ApprovalsPolicy.Scope ('run' default or 'day'), threaded through Registry/Gate.scopeKey; opt-in via policy.yaml, no behavior change by default
